title: Developmental Milestones
oneLiner: Structured assessment of motor, language, social, and cognitive skills to detect delay, red-flag serious underlying pathology, and enable early intervention.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Non-accidental head injury (NAHI) / abusive head trauma**  
  – *Single finding:* bulging fontanelle + retinal haemorrhages + unexplained regression (e.g., loss of smiling, poor feeding).  
  – Must be ruled out in any infant <12 months with sudden regression or unexplained collapse – check full septic screen (LP, bloods) + CT head + ophthalmology review.
- **Metabolic encephalopathy (e.g., maple syrup urine disease, urea cycle defect)**  
  – *Single finding:* encephalopathy (poor feeding, vomiting, seizures) + sweet/ketotic odour + acidosis.  
  – Send stat blood gas, glucose, ammonia, lactate, ketones – treat empirically with IV 10% dextrose and stop feeds pending specialist advice.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  – Parental concern: “not sitting”, “not talking”, “unable to do what other children their age do”.
  – Loss of already‑acquired skills (regression) – always pathological.
  – Asymmetric motor effort (e.g., hand preference before 18 months).
  – Poor visual fixing or following, limited response to sound.
  – Unusual behaviour (repetitive, lack of social smile, no joint attention).
- **Key exam findings:**
  – Growth centiles (weight, height, head circumference – plot serially).
  – Dysmorphic features, skin stigmata (café‑au‑lait spots, neurofibromas).
  – Neurological: tone (hypo‑ vs hypertonia), power, reflexes, persistence of primitive reflexes >6 months.
  – Vision: fixing/following, red‑reflex, assess ocular movements.
  – Hearing: startle to loud noise, response to voice, whisper test >3 years.
  – Height, weight, and OFC plotting – inspect for abnormal rise/fall crossing centiles.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  – Simple motor delay (constitutional) – normal tone, no asymmetry, normal social interaction.
  – Language delay ± hearing loss – no regression, attends well to sounds once hearing aids fitted.
  – Global developmental delay (all domains) – often cerebral palsy or genetic syndrome.
  – Autism spectrum disorder – poor social communication, repetitive behaviour, no regression of motor skills.
  – Metabolic / neurodegenerative (progressive encephalopathy with regression – key: loss of skills in a previously normal child).
- **Splitter:** *Regression* (loss of skills) = neurodegenerative/metabolic until proven otherwise; *no regression* = simple delay or static encephalopathy.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  – Plot all growth parameters (OFC, weight, height) on UK‑WHO centile charts.
  – Check red‑reflex test (Bruckner test) with ophthalmoscope.
  – Use validated screening tool: Ages & Stages Questionnaire (ASQ-3) or NICE‑recommended tool for the age.
  – If <6 months with collapse: blood gas, glucose, lactate, ammonia, LP if infection suspected.
- **Bloods:**
  – If regression + encephalopathy: ammonia, lactate, amino acids, organic acids, acylcarnitine profile, very long‑chain fatty acids.
  – If dysmorphic: microarray, specific genetic panel (e.g., Rett, Angelman).
  – If motor delay with hypotonia: creatinine kinase (Ducheme muscular dystrophy), thyroid function (congenital hypothyroidism).
- **Imaging:**
  – First‑line: MRI brain with contrast + spectroscopy (if metabolic/structural query).
  – Second‑line: CT head (if NAI suspected), skeletal survey (if NAI) before MRI.
- **Special tests:**
  – Hearing: otoacoustic emissions (OAE) or auditory brainstem response (ABR) – mandatory in isolated speech delay.
  – Vision: orthoptic assessment, RetCam if intraocular pathology suspected.
  – EEG: if seizures or regression (consider infantile spasms – hypsarrhythmia).
- **Never‑miss test:** Formal audiology assessment in any child with speech delay – amenable to early intervention. Also head circumference as a serial measurement – one off is not enough.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  – Suspected NAI: safeguarding referral + CT head + ophthalmology for retinal exam; do not confront family, focus on clinical needs.
  – Suspected metabolic crisis: IV 0.9% saline 10 mL/kg, IV 10% dextrose maintenance, stop enteral feeds, contact metabolic team on call.
  – If encephalopathy + fever: treat as meningitis/encephalitis empirically until proven otherwise (cefotaxime + aciclovir).
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  – Multidisciplinary team referral: paediatrician, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech & language therapy (SALT), audiology, ophthalmology, child development centre.
  – Start early intervention programme: Portage, Sure Start, local child development service.
  – Arrange genetic counselling and definitive metabolic workup if indicated.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  – Regular review at child development clinic (every 3‑6 months).
  – Educational support (Education, Health and Care Plan – EHCP).
  – Key worker assigned for family support.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  – Not applicable – developmental delay is rarely a reason for acute admission; manage underlying cause.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- *(No specific drugs for “developmental milestones” – treatable causes only)*
- Congenital hypothyroidism: levothyroxine – [check local policy] – do not delay.
- Dopamine‑responsive dystonia (SLC6A3 mutation): levodopa/carbidopa – discuss with neurology.
- Infantile spasms: vigabatrin or prednisolone – discuss with paediatric neurologist immediately.
- **Key contraindication:** Do not give carbamazepine or valproate in metabolic conditions (urea cycle defects, mitochondrial disease) without specialist advice.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving high‑dose steroids in a child with unsuspected infection or metabolic crisis – can cause rapid deterioration. Always check for sepsis first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  – Missed NAI leading to re‑injury or death.
  – Missed metabolic crisis resulting in encephalopathy, seizures, coma.
- **Chronic:**
  – Irreversible developmental deficits if early intervention delayed.
  – Parental anxiety and family breakdown without adequate support.
  – Missed treatable cause (e.g., congenital hypothyroidism, hearing loss) leading to permanent intellectual disability.
sections.Prognosis: - Highly variable by cause: simple motor delay → usually normal outcome by school age with physiotherapy. Global delay → lifelong impairment. Regression from metabolic disorder → often progressive decline and early mortality.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always plot head circumference on the same centile chart and compare with previous – an upward or downward crossing of ≥2 centiles is a red flag.
- Use the “Sandra’s rule” for regression: any loss of skills in a child previously meeting milestones → discuss with paediatric neurologist same day.
- Engage the parent as the expert – ask “What can your child do today that they couldn’t do a month ago?” – subtle regression often catches early.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- Never dismiss “he’ll grow out of it” without objective screening – use a validated tool.
- Do not forget hearing assessment in isolated speech delay – it’s the most common correctable cause.
- Do not ignore asymmetry of movement (hand preference <12 months is abnormal) – suspect hemiplegic cerebral palsy.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  – Child becomes unwell (fever, vomiting, altered consciousness, seizures).
  – Loss of any previously acquired skill (e.g., stops sitting, stops babbling).
  – New focal neurological signs (weakness, squint, drooling).
- **Follow‑up:** Routine – GP or health visitor review in 4 weeks if mild concern; if red flag present, refer to community paediatrician within 2 weeks; for regression – urgent outpatient paediatric neurology within 1 week.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ paediatric consultant immediately if:**
  – GCS <15 or acute encephalopathy.
  – Suspected NAI (call child protection team and senior paediatrician).
  – Suspected metabolic crisis (ammonia >150 µmol/L, lactate >4 mmol/L, persistent hypoglycaemia).
  – Seizures not controlled after two benzodiazepines.
- **ICU criteria:** Need for respiratory support, refractory status epilepticus, severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2).

rawMarkdown:
## Developmental Milestones
**One-liner:** Structured assessment of motor, language, social, and cognitive skills to detect delay, red-flag serious underlying pathology, and enable early intervention.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Non-accidental head injury (NAHI) / abusive head trauma**  
  – *Single finding:* bulging fontanelle + retinal haemorrhages + unexplained regression (e.g., loss of smiling, poor feeding).  
  – Must be ruled out in any infant <12 months with sudden regression or unexplained collapse – check full septic screen (LP, bloods) + CT head + ophthalmology review.
- **Metabolic encephalopathy (e.g., maple syrup urine disease, urea cycle defect)**  
  – *Single finding:* encephalopathy (poor feeding, vomiting, seizures) + sweet/ketotic odour + acidosis.  
  – Send stat blood gas, glucose, ammonia, lactate, ketones – treat empirically with IV 10% dextrose and stop feeds pending specialist advice.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  – Parental concern: “not sitting”, “not talking”, “unable to do what other children their age do”.
  – Loss of already‑acquired skills (regression) – always pathological.
  – Asymmetric motor effort (e.g., hand preference before 18 months).
  – Poor visual fixing or following, limited response to sound.
  – Unusual behaviour (repetitive, lack of social smile, no joint attention).
- **Key exam findings:**
  – Growth centiles (weight, height, head circumference – plot serially).
  – Dysmorphic features, skin stigmata (café‑au‑lait spots, neurofibromas).
  – Neurological: tone (hypo‑ vs hypertonia), power, reflexes, persistence of primitive reflexes >6 months.
  – Vision: fixing/following, red‑reflex, assess ocular movements.
  – Hearing: startle to loud noise, response to voice, whisper test >3 years.
  – Height, weight, and OFC plotting – inspect for abnormal rise/fall crossing centiles.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  – Simple motor delay (constitutional) – normal tone, no asymmetry, normal social interaction.
  – Language delay ± hearing loss – no regression, attends well to sounds once hearing aids fitted.
  – Global developmental delay (all domains) – often cerebral palsy or genetic syndrome.
  – Autism spectrum disorder – poor social communication, repetitive behaviour, no regression of motor skills.
  – Metabolic / neurodegenerative (progressive encephalopathy with regression – key: loss of skills in a previously normal child).
- **Splitter:** *Regression* (loss of skills) = neurodegenerative/metabolic until proven otherwise; *no regression* = simple delay or static encephalopathy.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  – Plot all growth parameters (OFC, weight, height) on UK‑WHO centile charts.
  – Check red‑reflex test (Bruckner test) with ophthalmoscope.
  – Use validated screening tool: Ages & Stages Questionnaire (ASQ-3) or NICE‑recommended tool for the age.
  – If <6 months with collapse: blood gas, glucose, lactate, ammonia, LP if infection suspected.
- **Bloods:**
  – If regression + encephalopathy: ammonia, lactate, amino acids, organic acids, acylcarnitine profile, very long‑chain fatty acids.
  – If dysmorphic: microarray, specific genetic panel (e.g., Rett, Angelman).
  – If motor delay with hypotonia: creatinine kinase (Ducheme muscular dystrophy), thyroid function (congenital hypothyroidism).
- **Imaging:**
  – First‑line: MRI brain with contrast + spectroscopy (if metabolic/structural query).
  – Second‑line: CT head (if NAI suspected), skeletal survey (if NAI) before MRI.
- **Special tests:**
  – Hearing: otoacoustic emissions (OAE) or auditory brainstem response (ABR) – mandatory in isolated speech delay.
  – Vision: orthoptic assessment, RetCam if intraocular pathology suspected.
  – EEG: if seizures or regression (consider infantile spasms – hypsarrhythmia).
- **Never‑miss test:** Formal audiology assessment in any child with speech delay – amenable to early intervention. Also head circumference as a serial measurement – one off is not enough.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  – Suspected NAI: safeguarding referral + CT head + ophthalmology for retinal exam; do not confront family, focus on clinical needs.
  – Suspected metabolic crisis: IV 0.9% saline 10 mL/kg, IV 10% dextrose maintenance, stop enteral feeds, contact metabolic team on call.
  – If encephalopathy + fever: treat as meningitis/encephalitis empirically until proven otherwise (cefotaxime + aciclovir).
- **Ward (first 24‑72h):**
  – Multidisciplinary team referral: paediatrician, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech & language therapy (SALT), audiology, ophthalmology, child development centre.
  – Start early intervention programme: Portage, Sure Start, local child development service.
  – Arrange genetic counselling and definitive metabolic workup if indicated.
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  – Regular review at child development clinic (every 3‑6 months).
  – Educational support (Education, Health and Care Plan – EHCP).
  – Key worker assigned for family support.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  – Not applicable – developmental delay is rarely a reason for acute admission; manage underlying cause.

### Drugs & Doses
- *(No specific drugs for “developmental milestones” – treatable causes only)*
- Congenital hypothyroidism: levothyroxine – [check local policy] – do not delay.
- Dopamine‑responsive dystonia (SLC6A3 mutation): levodopa/carbidopa – discuss with neurology.
- Infantile spasms: vigabatrin or prednisolone – discuss with paediatric neurologist immediately.
- **Key contraindication:** Do not give carbamazepine or valproate in metabolic conditions (urea cycle defects, mitochondrial disease) without specialist advice.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Giving high‑dose steroids in a child with unsuspected infection or metabolic crisis – can cause rapid deterioration. Always check for sepsis first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  – Missed NAI leading to re‑injury or death.
  – Missed metabolic crisis resulting in encephalopathy, seizures, coma.
- **Chronic:**
  – Irreversible developmental deficits if early intervention delayed.
  – Parental anxiety and family breakdown without adequate support.
  – Missed treatable cause (e.g., congenital hypothyroidism, hearing loss) leading to permanent intellectual disability.

### Prognosis
- Highly variable by cause: simple motor delay → usually normal outcome by school age with physiotherapy. Global delay → lifelong impairment. Regression from metabolic disorder → often progressive decline and early mortality.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always plot head circumference on the same centile chart and compare with previous – an upward or downward crossing of ≥2 centiles is a red flag.
- Use the “Sandra’s rule” for regression: any loss of skills in a child previously meeting milestones → discuss with paediatric neurologist same day.
- Engage the parent as the expert – ask “What can your child do today that they couldn’t do a month ago?” – subtle regression often catches early.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- Never dismiss “he’ll grow out of it” without objective screening – use a validated tool.
- Do not forget hearing assessment in isolated speech delay – it’s the most common correctable cause.
- Do not ignore asymmetry of movement (hand preference <12 months is abnormal) – suspect hemiplegic cerebral palsy.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  – Child becomes unwell (fever, vomiting, altered consciousness, seizures).
  – Loss of any previously acquired skill (e.g., stops sitting, stops babbling).
  – New focal neurological signs (weakness, squint, drooling).
- **Follow‑up:** Routine – GP or health visitor review in 4 weeks if mild concern; if red flag present, refer to community paediatrician within 2 weeks; for regression – urgent outpatient paediatric neurology within 1 week.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ paediatric consultant immediately if:**
  – GCS <15 or acute encephalopathy.
  – Suspected NAI (call child protection team and senior paediatrician).
  – Suspected metabolic crisis (ammonia >150 µmol/L, lactate >4 mmol/L, persistent hypoglycaemia).
  – Seizures not controlled after two benzodiazepines.
- **ICU criteria:** Need for respiratory support, refractory status epilepticus, severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2).
